topic: Pre-integration review of Registry Framing Audit (three-part placement: wins.json schema extension + Part 4.2 subsection + Timestamp Error tab callout)
our_argument_summary: EXP-183 proposes a three-part coordinated placement: (a) extend data/wins.json schema with six author_* fields populated by the analyst from the dome API layer (claim_index.json, methodology.json, registry_integrity_notes); (b) add a new Part 4 subsection 4.2 Registry Framing Audit hosting six registry-level aggregate gaps in a table; (c) add a short methodology-related callout+link in the Timestamp Error tab. Thesis: the dome author shipped machine-readable concessions (5-class taxonomy, strict_prospective_confirmed subset, registry_exclusion_cap, display-level bookkeeping label, pending_independent_verification for WIN-058) that the visible homepage/scorecard/live dashboard has not caught up to; the proposed architecture catalogs the divergence in a self-healing, data-bound way.

proposal_analysis.architecture_assessment: Sound. Per-WIN data-model extension (schema) + aggregate prose section + cross-link in canary tab is the right separation of concerns. Schema-driven rendering is the correct pattern for avoiding hardcoded counts (our own rule: never hardcode a count that can be derived).
proposal_analysis.verdict_flip_plausibility: Not applicable — this is a structural framing expansion, not a WIN verdict revision. The EXP does not flip any existing verdict. It exposes author-stated metadata alongside our verdicts, allowing side-by-side comparison. Readers might infer a different weight than before, but that is appropriate for genuinely new evidence.
proposal_analysis.new_tag_naming: The schema field prefix author_* is ambiguous. In a file we own (wins.json), author reads naturally as us (the review authors) — the reader will default to the nearest antecedent. Recommend renaming to dome_author_* or domesite_* to remove the ambiguity, OR nesting under a single dome_self_rating object to group the fields semantically (matches the existing code_analysis nested-object pattern).
proposal_analysis.alternatives_considered: EXP enumerates A/B/C/D rejected options with rejection reasons. The three-part PRIMARY/PRIMARY_COMPANION/PRIMARY_MINOR split is defensible. However, the EXP does not discuss the simplest alternative: patch the visible headline directly to reconcile 10/9/1 (which is what we would ask the author to do). Our review cannot do that for him, but the audit could frame as monitor what the author does next rather than catalog him forever.
proposal_analysis.unknowns_acknowledged: EXP lists six anticipated objections and seven counterpoint-considered items. Coverage of the objection space is strong. Missing: explicit treatment of the section numbering collision (it is flagged in the priority-queue.json reason, not in the EXP itself), the render-lag alternative for 10/9/1 (dismissed too quickly), and the 60-WIN empty-state repetition effect.

holes_found.description: Section numbering collision: the EXP proposes 4.2 Registry Framing Audit but Part 4 already has a 4.2 The August 2026 Eclipse subsection (id p4-42-1 in data/sections.json). Adding a second 4.2 would either (a) force renumbering 4.2->4.3, 4.3->4.4, 4.4->4.5, 4.5->4.6 and update every inbound anchor link, or (b) place the new content at 4.6 and break the EXP self-descriptions. The EXP names the section 4.2 throughout (heading H2, TLDR, cross-references from replacement_html, id=registry-framing-audit, callout link #registry-framing-audit). The priority-queue reason flags this conflict but the EXP does not propose a placement rule. Unblocker required before integration.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a placement_constraints item specifying the renumbering plan. Options: (i) place at 4.6 and retitle throughout the EXP; (ii) place at 4.1b and retitle; (iii) renumber existing 4.2-4.5 to 4.3-4.6 with full anchor/link migration audit. Option (i) or (ii) has lowest blast radius. The decider should not have to infer the renumbering scheme.

holes_found.description: Quantitative claim 10/9/1 prospective_confirmed count drift is partially unverified. The EXP calls this the sharpest instance in the TLDR and opens the new GAP-PROSPECTIVE-DRIFT-10-9-1 row on it. I verified 10 (ai_manifest.json / daily_review.json, confirmed in chg-20260417-1620-003: Registry stands at 69 confirmed, 4 refined, and 10 prospective). But the EXP does not produce an exact file-line citation for (a) visible grid shows 9 or (b) strict_prospective_confirmed counts 1 in claim_index.json. The change files do not pin those two numbers. The EXP even says in completion_signal: ISS-NEXT prospective_confirmed count drift 10/9/1 — newly-discovered aggregate gap. We should not publish a newly-discovered quantitative gap as the TLDR anchor until all three values are cited with exact source locations (path + anchor or jq-path).
holes_found.recommendation: Before integration: (a) cite exact URL/anchor for the visible grid 9 count (e.g., predictions/index.html#prospective-confirmed-grid) and (b) cite exact jq-path or field for strict_prospective_confirmed counts 1 in claim_index.json. If either cannot be cited, soften the table row (remove the 1 or reframe as 10/9 drift only) and remove the three-layer language from the TLDR. The three-layer framing is the strongest rhetorical point; it must be the most verifiable.

holes_found.description: live.html footer 67 confirmed vs homepage 69 confirmed — the render-lag counterargument is not closed. The EXP dismisses render-lag globally by saying the drift has three values, not two — a rendering lag should produce old and new, not three different live values. That logic applies to the 10/9/1 row. It does NOT apply to 67/69, which is exactly two values. A reasonable defender reply: the new live.html was added in commit a57e731 (chg-20260417-1620-003) with 104 new lines; the footer was hardcoded or templated in that new file; 67 is simply a stale hand-typed number from before WIN-057b and WIN-068 were onboarded. If the author fixes the footer to 69 tomorrow, our row collapses to a 24-hour old bug. Publishing a cache-consistency bug as a framing contradiction over-reaches.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop GAP-LIVE-67-FOOTER from the table until it has persisted for one more poll cycle, or (b) reframe it as a low-severity consistency note alongside the six registry-level concessions rather than a framing contradiction equivalent to the 5-class taxonomy coverage gap. Severity calibration matters because the table treats all six rows as equals.

holes_found.description: Schema field naming author_* is ambiguous in the consumer context. wins.json is OUR file, so author reads most naturally as review authors (us). A reader scanning author_claim_class on a WIN card will guess it is our classification before noticing it is the dome author. This is the opposite of the clarity goal. The analogous existing pattern in wins.json is code_analysis (nested object with dedicated prefix for a specific source). Recommend either dome_author_claim_class (explicit prefix) or nest under dome_self_rating: { claim_class, registry_status, strict_prospective_confirmed, visible_badge, integrity_note, last_checked } to match the code_analysis precedent.
holes_found.recommendation: Rename fields before freezing the schema. Cheapest path: rename prefix author_ -> dome_ on all six fields (dome_claim_class, dome_registry_status, etc.). Better path: nest under dome_self_rating object — matches existing code_analysis convention and keeps top-level wins.json row clean. Do this BEFORE analyst backfills 12 rows; renaming later is harder.

holes_found.description: Self-healing is overclaimed. The EXP repeatedly frames the audit as self-healing — if the author propagates API concessions to the visible layer, the gaps close automatically. True for the per-WIN surface integrity notes (GAP-WIN-058/058b/062 resolve when author removes the collision in wins.html). True for 10/9/1 drift. But GAP-TAXONOMY-COVERAGE (9 of 69+ classified) only closes if the author classifies 60 more WINs — that is substantial manual effort, not propagation of existing rigor. The same applies to GAP-CLAIM-CLASS-9. The EXP telling readers this audit is designed to shrink as the author propagates his API-level rigor to the visible layer sets an expectation that two of six rows cannot actually meet on reasonable timescales.
holes_found.recommendation: In the kernel-of-truth and Canary paragraphs, distinguish rows that close on propagation (rendering fixes, headline edits) from rows that close only on additional author work (taxonomy application to 60 more WINs). This is a small wording change that keeps the self-healing framing for the rows where it applies and acknowledges the others as longer-horizon trackers.

holes_found.description: Render-lag dismissal for 10/9/1 is too fast. The EXP argues: the drift has three values, not two — a rendering lag should produce old and new, not three different live values. But three different publication surfaces with three different update cadences (manual author edit of ai_manifest.json, weekly build script for visible grid, hand-curated claim_index.json) can hold three different stale values simultaneously without any being current. The three-values test does not rule out multi-surface render lag; it only rules out a single two-stage pipeline. A dome defender can and will use this. The EXP own counterpoint should handle the multi-pipeline case.
holes_found.recommendation: In the anticipated_objections block, replace the three-values-not-two argument with a stronger one: whichever of 10, 9, or 1 is correct, the other two are wrong on surfaces the author owns and publishes to auditors. A single corrected number eliminates two-thirds of the divergence. The burden of correction is one number, not three. That is a much harder counterpunch for a defender to take.

holes_found.description: TLDR conflates prospective_confirmed with the headline. The TLDR says most sharply, a three-layer disagreement on the headline prospective_confirmed count itself (10 / 9 / 1). The dome headline is 69 Confirmed / 94.5% accuracy, not prospective_confirmed. Prospective_confirmed is a sub-count of Confirmed. A careful reader will notice the conflation on first pass and it weakens the TLDR credibility. Section prose below handles this correctly (the 10/9/1 row is labeled disagree on the headline prospective_confirmed count, reserving headline for the 94.5%/69 claim via GAP-HEADLINE-94.5). Only the TLDR is imprecise.
holes_found.recommendation: Revise TLDR to: most sharply, a three-layer disagreement on the prospective_confirmed sub-count (10 / 9 / 1) and a visible 94.5% accuracy headline that the API layer labels display-level bookkeeping. This keeps the two strongest hooks and uses the right label for each.

holes_found.description: 60-WIN empty-state repetition effect is under-addressed. Rendering (author has not classified this claim) on 60 of 69 WIN detail cards — even with muted styling and fact-of-record tone — is a repeated micro-claim that reads as incomplete taxonomy rollout framing. The counterpoint section acknowledges this (could read as nagging) and mitigates with muted styling, but the mitigation may not survive contact with the rendered output. We criticize the dome model for hardcoding static counts and for strawman rhetoric; we should hold ourselves to the same standard and not rely on visual weight to make a point that the prose could make once in the Registry Framing Audit section.
holes_found.recommendation: Strong recommendation: render the author_claim_class line on a WIN detail card ONLY when the field is populated. If the field is null, omit the line (do not display a stub). The aggregate story 9 of 69+ classified belongs in the Part 4.2 section, not on 60 WIN cards. This also removes the decider-side concern about muted styling guidelines that must be enforced across 60 rows.

holes_found.description: Internal count inconsistency: EXP text sometimes says 6 (registry-level concessions), sometimes 8-9 (instances as of 2026-04-18). The registry_level_gaps array has 6 entries (correct). The win_level_gaps array has 4 entries (3 WINs with integrity notes + 1 aggregate for the 9 classified). The other_side_caveats paragraph refers to 8-9 instances compiled today and 6 independently-verifiable concessions. The breaking news hook says 6 machine-readable rigor upgrades. The newsworthy field says six registry-level concessions. Readers comparing numbers will find 4 + 6 = 10, not 6 or 8-9.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one accurate aggregate framing and use it throughout: e.g., six registry-level gaps + four per-WIN gaps = 10 total tracked instances. Or: six visible-layer / API-layer divergences on aggregates, plus three individual WIN overlaps. Either is fine; drifting among three numbers is the issue.

holes_found.description: 23 inbound onclick='showTab(timestamp)' references (grep-verified) — I count 22 in docs/index.html and 7 in data/sections.json (which expand on build to the 22 in docs/index.html). The approximately correct claim is 22 references in the built HTML, not 23. Not load-bearing but cite accuracy matters because the EXP uses this count to justify preserving the tab id.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 23 onclick... references to approximately 22 references (22 in docs/index.html built from 7 in sections.json) or re-run the grep and use the exact current number. The argument (preserve the tab id) is unaffected; only the number is off.

holes_found.description: The EXP assumes Part 4.1 (from EXP-182) is stable and can be cross-referenced. Queue item 176 (SEC-4.1-post-EXP-182) has not been curmudgeon-reviewed yet — it is behind this item in the queue. If the Part 4.1 review surfaces a major hole, the replacement_html cross-references to Section 4.1 divided audience H3 may need revision. This is not a blocker but a coordination risk.
holes_found.recommendation: Decider should sequence integration: (a) process Part 4.1 curmudgeon review (queue_id 176) first; (b) if Part 4.1 needs revision, defer EXP-183 integration or pin the cross-reference to a stable anchor. If Part 4.1 review comes back clean, proceed with EXP-183 as-is on this dimension.

holes_found.description: Load-bearing headline claim is asserted without evidence. The EXP says the headline is the load-bearing public claim for the model reception — on social shares, citation, casual reading. No evidence is cited (no share counts, no citation list, no reader-analytics). A defender can ask for proof; we have none beyond inference. This is not wrong but it reads stronger than the evidence supports.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to the headline is the most public-facing surface — it is what index.html shows above the fold and what the homepage sc-cards propagate, or cite a specific instance (a social-share HTML meta tag, a cite-able press mention, etc.) if one exists. Inference-only framing is fine if stated as inference.

stronger_arguments.description: The cleanest falsifiable prediction from this audit: within 30 days of the EXP-183 publication, does the homepage visible layer adopt ANY of the API-layer concessions? If the author reads this review and patches even one surface, that confirms reactive patching. If the author removes a concession instead (e.g., deletes registry_integrity_notes), that is a stronger canary than the OTS wrong-side error. Adding this explicit time-boxed test to the Canary and self-healing property paragraph upgrades it from a descriptive claim to a testable prediction.
sources: Existing canary framing on the Timestamp Error tab
Poller agent existing edit-history monitoring of predictions/*.json

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest counterargument to our audit is the author iterating — API first, visible later is industry standard (arxiv preprint vs press release). A stronger rebuttal: most publications that show API/visible divergence update the visible layer within weeks, not indefinitely. If the divergence persists at 30/60/90 days, iteration is no longer the best explanation. The EXP mentions dated evidence (as of 2026-04-18) but does not commit to a revisit cadence. Adding a schedule (poller revisits this audit at +30 days, analyst updates the table) converts a snapshot audit into a durable measurement.

The display-level bookkeeping concession (ai_manifest.json registry_semantics) is the single strongest instance in the table and should be the opening kill-shot of the section, not the fifth row. A reader who only reads the first row will carry away the weakest framing (94.5% vs strict=1, which is definitional-difference territory). A reader who gets to row 5 takes away the strongest framing (author himself says the visible layer is bookkeeping). Reorder: put registry_semantics first, headline_drift second, prospective-count-drift third, taxonomy-coverage fourth, live.html footer fifth (if kept), CVaR-cap last.
sources: Rhetorical principle: lead with the concession that requires zero interpretation

kernel_of_truth.description: The fundamental insight is sharp and well-founded: the dome author has shipped machine-readable concessions during the April 2026 rigor push (claim taxonomy, registry_integrity_notes for WIN-058/058b/062, strict_prospective_confirmed subset, CVaR exclusion cap, display-level bookkeeping label) that the visible homepage/scorecard has not caught up to. This IS a testable, concrete divergence pattern. The proposed architecture (schema extension + Part 4.2 subsection + Timestamp tab callout) is the right shape: data-bound per-WIN surfaces, aggregate prose for registry-level gaps, narrow cross-link from the OTS canary tab. The author has genuinely conceded items our review flagged (WIN-058/062 collisions, OTS scope) and the credit-paragraph in the replacement_html is appropriate.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A — this is not a WIN verdict review. The EXP architecture survives all of my holes; the holes are about specific numbers, specific naming choices, and specific rhetorical tightening. Approving integration with the hole-list as conditions is the correct disposition, not rejection.

blocking_conditions: HOLE-1 (section numbering collision — needs explicit placement rule)
HOLE-2 (10/9/1 drift needs exact file citations for 9 and 1)
HOLE-4 (author_* prefix rename or nest under dome_self_rating before schema freeze)
non_blocking_but_requested: HOLE-5, HOLE-6 (self-healing framing precision + render-lag rebuttal strengthening)
HOLE-8 (omit null author_claim_class rendering on WIN cards)
nice_to_have: Stronger_arguments items 1-3 (time-boxed canary, revisit cadence, row reorder)

cross_wins_consistency.consistency_check: EXP-181 already integrated per-WIN acknowledge-without-fix paragraphs for WIN-058, WIN-058b, WIN-062. EXP-183 adds structured fields (dome_author_registry_integrity_note, dome_author_registry_status) that render the same information as data-bound UI alongside the EXP-181 prose. Risk: redundant rendering if both the inline paragraph and the structured block are shown on the same card. Recommendation: decide ownership — either EXP-181 paragraph OR EXP-183 structured block, not both. The structured block is more scalable (handles future integrity notes automatically); the EXP-181 paragraph is more prose-integrated. Cleanest path: EXP-183 structured block replaces the EXP-181 hand-written paragraph on the three affected WIN cards, with EXP-181 prose merged into a shared template that the structured block renders. This requires a decider-level integration decision the EXP does not currently flag.
cross_wins_consistency.orphan_risk: If EXP-183 lands and EXP-181 prose is not updated, WIN-058/058b/062 cards show duplicate information in two different blocks. Mitigation: integration_notes already says EXP-181 is complete in the tracker; the decider should NOT re-touch EXP-181 as part of this integration. But that is the wrong guidance for the duplicate-render problem. Revised integration_notes should require: replace EXP-181 integrity-note paragraph with EXP-183 structured block render on the three affected cards.

verified_externally: prospective_confirmed = 10 in ai_manifest.json / daily_review.json (confirmed via chg-20260417-1620-003 which quotes: Registry stands at 69 confirmed, 4 refined, and 10 prospective)

methodology.json 5-class taxonomy exists and names the five classes prospective_confirmed, retrospective_structural, supportive_nonunique, pending_contested, open_refinement (confirmed via chg-20260417-1620-001 verbatim quote)
ai_manifest.json registry_semantics = display-level bookkeeping (confirmed via chg-20260417-1620-005 verbatim quote)
registry_integrity_notes for WIN-058/058b/062 in claim_index.json (confirmed via chg-20260417-1620-004 verbatim quote)
WIN-058 visible badge Confirmed + honest-caveats line pending independent verification (confirmed via EXP-183 new_evidence_added block)
unverified_and_should_be: visible grid shows 9 prospective-confirmed items — EXP does not cite the exact predictions/index.html anchor or grid-filter parameter. Before publication, an analyst should produce a URL that deep-links to the count.

strict_prospective_confirmed counts 1 in claim_index.json — EXP does not cite the exact jq-path or entry structure. Before publication, an analyst should produce entries[] | select(.strict_prospective_confirmed == true) | length with the file SHA it was run against.
live.html footer reads 67 confirmed — EXP does not cite file SHA or line number. Poll chg-20260417-1620-003 mentions live.html as a new file (+104 lines) but does not quote 67.
CVaR registry_exclusion_cap = 6 location — EXP attributes to ai_manifest.json / methodology.json but does not specify which of the two holds the authoritative value.
quantitative_verification.inbound_tab_references: EXP claims 23 onclick='showTab(timestamp)' references grep-verified. Curmudgeon count: 22 in docs/index.html + 7 in data/sections.json (which build to the 22). Not load-bearing; update to 22 references in built HTML (7 in sections.json).

advocate_mode.prompt: Steelman the EXP critics. How would a sharp reviewer demolish it?
advocate_mode.critic_attack_1: You are leveraging the author rigor improvements against him, then claiming credit for noticing. The author publishes honest-caveats, you take the caveats and weaponize them.
advocate_mode.response_1: Credited in the kernel-of-truth paragraph. The critique is narrower: API concessions have not propagated to the visible headline. That is a specific, testable gap that closes automatically when the author reconciles. If we are wrong, the audit closes itself. Advantage: falsifiable, asymmetric risk.
advocate_mode.critic_attack_2: You catalog six instances and call it a pattern. In a 69-claim model with multiple publication surfaces, six divergences is not a pattern — it is a housekeeping debt. Pick any complex site on the web and you will find six inconsistencies.
advocate_mode.response_2: Weaker counter. The defender is right that six is not automatically a pattern. Our response has to be: (a) the six are concentrated on the highest-visibility quantities — the headline count, the prospective_confirmed sub-count, the 5-class taxonomy; not on layout or typography; (b) all six run the same direction (API more rigorous, visible less rigorous), which is not random. If the six were a 2-3-1 mix of directions, defender wins; because they are 6-0 in the same direction, we win. The EXP should state this symmetry argument explicitly.
advocate_mode.critic_attack_3: The self-healing framing is condescending — you assume the author is trying to hide things and will fix them when caught.
advocate_mode.response_3: The EXP repeatedly frames the audit as a progress tracker / running inventory, not as accusing-until-proven-innocent. The revision_notes specifically flag do not over-promise coverage completeness. The running catalog language is adequate mitigation, but the Canary and self-healing property section title leans into the very framing this attack targets. Recommend retitling that subsection to Running catalog — how it closes. Same mechanism, neutral language.

citations_verified: EXP-181 status (already integrated; cross-referenced by EXP-183)
EXP-182 Part 4.1 restructure (integrated commit 191a3d4; cross-referenced by EXP-183)
citations_failed: visible grid shows 9 — no file path or anchor cited
strict_prospective_confirmed counts 1 — no jq-path or entry SHA cited
live.html footer 67 — no line number cited
23 inbound timestamp tab references — count is approximately 22, not 23
citations_unchecked: HNOTE-API-HUMAN-GAPS-001 (EXP human_note_source) — did not read upstream
honest-caveats section WIN-058 pending independent verification — text exists per EXP new_evidence_added but line number not verified

deeper_analysis: EXP-183 is an unusually well-structured proposal — the three-part placement correctly separates per-WIN data (schema) from aggregate prose (Part 4.2) from cross-reference (Timestamp tab callout). The architecture is not in question. What needs work is specificity in three places: the section numbering (HOLE-1), the unverified counts in the strongest rhetorical row (HOLE-2), and the schema field naming (HOLE-4). These are the three blocking conditions. The remaining holes are rhetorical tightening (HOLE-3, HOLE-5 through HOLE-9) and citation precision (HOLE-10 through HOLE-12). The stronger_arguments items — especially the 30/60/90-day time-boxed canary test, the row reorder to put display-level bookkeeping first, and the 6-0 directional symmetry argument against the housekeeping-debt counter — would substantially raise the ceiling of the section if adopted. Integration disposition: integrate_with_conditions. Decider should hold the integration pending the three blocking items; then EXP-183 is ready to ship. Coordination risk: queue_id 176 (Part 4.1 post-EXP-182) is ahead in the queue and shares a tab with this item. Sequence them. I do not recommend rejecting the proposal; the divergence pattern is genuine and the architecture is sound.
confidence_reasoning: 0.75 confidence — high on architecture (the three-part placement is clearly right), high on the three blocking conditions being real (section conflict is objectively demonstrable; schema naming is a readable English ambiguity; unverified counts are enumerable). Medium-low on the stronger_arguments items because they are rhetorical improvements rather than falsifiable claims. Where I am least confident: (a) whether the EXP-181 / EXP-183 duplicate-render problem is as severe as I flag, since I did not read the current EXP-181 rendering on the three affected WIN cards; (b) whether GAP-LIVE-67-FOOTER should be dropped entirely or just re-severitied, since I did not directly read live.html to see how prominent the 67 is. Both are local to specific holes and do not shift the overall integrate_with_conditions disposition.
meta_notes: The pre-integration review pattern (decider queues expansion-draft, curmudgeon reviews, decider integrates) is working as designed. This is the correct place to catch HOLE-1, HOLE-2, HOLE-4 before they ship.

EXP-183 revision history (revision_notes 2026-04-18) shows the analyst already incorporated one round of user feedback (rejected original Option B that overloaded the Timestamp tab). The current proposal is the second revision. The pattern of holes — specific factual citations, naming ambiguity, over-claiming — is the normal tail of a substantive proposal, not a sign of structural weakness.

One non-blocking observation for the decider: this review does not opine on the Part 4.2 title Registry Framing Audit. The title is clear and testable. If HOLE-1 resolves by renumbering to 4.6, the title should remain stable.
